steps.title: Follow the source of the genres
steps.content: Mooney assembled Cherokee oral stories from elders and then categorized those stories into genres. One of his categories was "wonder stories," a label he used for stories with detailed descriptions of geographic locations in the Cherokee homeland. The key qualification comes near the end: Cherokee people are not known to have applied genre divisions to their stories before Mooney's work. The conclusion after "therefore" must follow from that gap between Mooney's later classification system and the elders' own known practice.

steps.title: Eliminate unsupported conclusions
steps.content: Choice A goes beyond the text by speculating about other traditional stories that the elders did not share with Mooney. Choice B shifts to whether the elders believed the geographic details were accurate, but the passage is about genre divisions, not accuracy. Choice C introduces uncertainty about Long's influence on Mooney's categories, which the passage does not discuss. The supported conclusion is narrower: because the genre marker came from Mooney and not from any known Cherokee genre system, there is no reason to assume the elders would have accepted geographic specificity as marking a different kind of story. The answer is D.
